content.full_text:
McCUTCHEN, DOYLE, BROWN & ENERSEN
COUNSELORS AT LAW

SAN FRANCISCO                    1855 OLYMPIC BOULEVARD, THIRD FLOOR                    WALNUT CREEK OFFICE
SAN JOSE                         POST OFFICE BOX V                                      TELEX 340817 MACPAG SFO
COSTA MESA                                                                              FACSIMILE GI, II AND III
WASHINGTON, D.C.               WALNUT CREEK, CALIFORNIA 94596                          (415) 930-2390
SHANGHAI
TAIPEI                          TELEPHONE (415) 937-8000


                                                    March 6, 1990


FEDERAL EXPRESS


John W. Wallace, Esq.
Legal Division
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                    Conflict of Interest of
                                    Napa County LAFCO Commissioner
                                    Our File No. 89000-001

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     We represent the American Canyon Incorporation
Committee in relation to its proposal to incorporate the
American Canyon area of Napa County. That proposal is
currently before the Napa County Local Agency Formation
Commission ("LAFCO") for review, prior to being sent to the
voters for action. It is our understanding that the Napa
County Counsel's office has requested your assistance
concerning the California Political Reform Act (the "Act") as
it may apply to LAFCO Commissioner John Mikolajcik and his
involvement in LAFCO actions on that incorporation proposal.

     For the reasons set forth below, we believe that no
conflict of interest exists and that Commissioner Mikolajcik
should be allowed to participate in all LAFCO actions relative
to the incorporation.

I.         SUMMARY

     Commissioner Mikolajcik should be permitted to
participate in the LAFCO decision regarding the American Canyon
incorporation. LAFCO does not have the power to organize an
unincorporated area into a city. LAFCO approval merely allows
the incorporation process to continue. Ultimately, the
affected voters in the area will either approve or disapprove

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990
Page 2

the incorporation. Further, LAFCO does not possess land use
policy or regulatory powers (the powers which materially effect
financial interests), it merely takes actions which may lead to
the establishment of such policies and regulations by other
governmental entities. Whatever financial effects may flow to
the Commissioner's property or business are mere possibilities
at this stage and cannot be considered reasonably foreseeable,
are not material, and are not distinguishable from effects on
the public generally. Thus, the Act does not require that the
Commissioner disqualify himself.

II.       ANALYSIS

       A.    Background.

As the County Counsel's office outlined in its letter,
our client has submitted a petition to the Napa County LAFCO
proposing the incorporation of the American Canyon area of the
County pursuant to the Cortese-Knox Local Government
Reorganization Act of 1985. Government Code¹ Section 56000
et seq. The Commissioner represents and lives in the American
Canyon area, where he owns a residence, certain undeveloped
property and a nursery business.

       B.    Applicable Standard.

The Political Reform Act provides that "no public
official at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in the making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."
Section 87100.

However, a number of elements must exist before the
Act requires a public official to disqualify himself from
participation in a governmental decision: First, it must be
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have
a financial effect. Second, the anticipated financial effect
must be on a financial interest of the official, and it must be
material. And third, the anticipated financial effect must be
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. See,
In re Thorner, 1 FPPC 198, 202 (1975).

¹ All section references are to the California Government
Code unless otherwise indicated.

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990
Page 3

Each of these elements are discussed below.

1. Foreseeability.

Before the Commissioner must disqualify himself from
voting on the proposed American Canyon incorporation, it must
be "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a
material effect on one of these interests. The preliminary
nature of the LAFCO decision and the particular facts
surrounding the Commissioner's situation suggest that no
material financial effect on these interests is reasonably
foreseeable.

Whether an effect is foreseeable depends on the
particular facts and circumstances of each situation. See,
Thorner, supra. A financial effect is considered reasonably
foreseeable when there is a substantial likelihood or
probability that it will occur. Certainty is not required;
however, if the financial effect is only a mere possibility, it
is not reasonably foreseeable. Id.

An understanding of the incorporation process and
LAFCO's limited powers is key to understanding why the LAFCO
decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable effect on the
Commissioner's financial interests. First, LAFCO's decision
will not be the final determinant of the American Canyon
incorporation. LAFCO does not have the power to organize an
unincorporated area into a city. Instead, LAFCO's approval
only serves to forward the proposal to the "conducting
authority," which then must determine if the proposed
incorporation should be placed before the effected citizens.
See, Sections 57077, 57078. The voters residing in the
proposed incorporation area render the final decision on
incorporation.

Second, LAFCO has no power over existing or subsequent
land use policies and regulations regarding the proposed
incorporation area (or any other area). Section 56375(a)(2).
If the proposed incorporation is rejected by the voters,
control over land use will remain with the Board of
Supervisors; if the incorporation is approved, the new city
council will exercise that power. In either case, LAFCO will
not play a policy-making role regarding land use issues.

Because the voters, not LAFCO, will determine whether
the new city is established, and because LAFCO has no power to
directly control land use, any financial effect which may indirectly

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990
Page 4

flow from LAFCO's decision on the incorporation, is a "mere
possibility" and too remote to be considered reasonably
foreseeable.

2.     Financial Interest and Materiality.

     Under the Act, a decision's financial effect is
considered "material" in one of two ways.  First,
Section 18702.1 of Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations ("Regs.") lists specific situations which have
been generally determined to be material.  Second, when
Section 18702.1 cannot be applied, a decision will be said to
have a material effect "if the decision will have a significant
effect" on the official or his financial interests.  Regs.
Section 18702(b).

          a.     Real Property.

     Section 18702.1(a)(3) outlines situations where the
decision will materially affect the official's interests in
real property.  This section specifically mentions decisions
involving annexation or deannexation but fails to include those
involving incorporations.  A likely explanation for the
omission is that incorporations typically involve a much larger
area than annexations and thus involve a significant segment of
the public by definition.  In addition, an incorporation
requires a vote of the affected population.  Thus, the effect
of any potential conflict can be rectified by the voters.

     Assuming that the LAFCO decision is not covered by
Section 18702.1 and thus its effect is not material by
definition, the decision's effect is material only if it will
have a "significant effect" on the Commissioner's residence or
undeveloped parcel.  Again, because LAFCO cannot incorporate
American Canyon or control land use within the American Canyon
area, its decision will not significantly effect the
Commissioner's real property interests.  Additional actions on
the part of American Canyon residents and a city council not
yet in existence must occur before the possibility of a
significant effect will occur.

          b.     Business Investment.

     The Commissioner's nursery business is a business
entity in which the Commissioner has a direct investment of
more than $1,000 and in which he has a position of management.
Section 18702.1(a)(2).  However, as discussed below, the

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990
Page 5

anticipated effect of the decision will not be distinguishable
from the public generally because the nursery is but one of
many retail businesses located in the proposed incorporation
area. Therefore, no conflict exists.

3. Public Generally Distinction.

Finally, before the Act requires an official to
disqualify himself, the "decision's anticipated financial
effect on the official's financial interest must be
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally."
Thorner, at 202, emphasis in original. Thus, even if you
determine that the LAFCO decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable and material effect on the Commissioner's property
and business, a conflict will not exist if the effect is not
distinguishable from the public generally.

a. Real Property.

The Commissioner owns a single family residence and
some vacant land in the proposed incorporation area. However,
a conflict does not exist. The Commissioner, as a landowner,
is a member of a group which constitutes a "significant
segment" of the public. Thus the effect of the LAFCO decision
on his property will not be distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally. In re Owen, 2 FPPC 77 (1976).

In Owen, a planning commissioner, who owned a home on
the border of a core area, was allowed to participate in
decisions involving the core area's specific land use plan.
Id. at 78. The FPPC reasoned that:

While certain aspects of the plan may operate to
increase the value of this home, either as residential
or commercial property, it is apparent that the plan
will have a "substantially similar" effect on numerous
other residential properties, perhaps throughout the
entire city as well as those near the core area.

Id. at 81.

In the instant case, while LAFCO's decision may
operate to increase the value of the Commissioner's land,
either as residential or commercial property, it will have a
substantially similar effect on all other properties and
property owners in the proposed incorporation area (and
possibly outside American Canyon as well).

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990
Page 6

b.     The Nursery.

     Again, the reasoning in Owen is controlling. In Owen,
the FPPC also determined that a council member, a retail
merchant, could participate in the adoption of a specific land
use plan for the City of Davis even though his store was
located in the plan's core area. The opinion stated that
"merchants constitute a major part of what is generally
regarded as the business community and taken as a whole, may
reasonably be regarded as 'the public generally' within the
meaning of Section 87103 and our regulations." Id. at 82. It
is important to note that the core area merchants did not
represent all merchants in the City of Davis. The FPPC also
recognized that there was no indication that the plan would
have a particularly beneficial or harmful effect on the council
member's particular business. Ibid.

     Under the reasoning of Owen, the Commissioner's
nursery falls within "the public generally" distinction. The
American Canyon business community represents "the public
generally;" very little retail business exists outside the
proposed incorporation area and a significant number of retail
businesses exist within the proposed borders. In addition,
there is no indication that the LAFCO decision will affect the
Commissioner's nursery any differently than the other
businesses. Finally, the effect of the possible incorporation
on the Commissioner's nursery business, an existing and ongoing
concern, is likely to be minimal.

     III.     CONCLUSION

     Commissioner Mikolajcik should be permitted to
participate in the LAFCO decision regarding the American Canyon
incorporation. LAFCO does not have the power to organize an
unincorporated area into a city. LAFCO approval merely allows
the incorporation process to continue. Ultimately, the
affected voters in the area will either approve or disapprove
the incorporation. Further, LAFCO does not possess land use
policy or regulatory powers (the powers which materially effect
financial interests); it merely takes actions which may lead to
the establishment of such policies and regulations by other
governmental entities. Whatever financial effects may flow to
the Commissioner's property or business are mere possibilities
at this stage and cannot be considered reasonably foreseeable,
are not material, and are not distinguishable from effects on
the public generally. Thus, the Act does not require that the
Commissioner disqualify himself.

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990
Page 7

We hope this letter will be of assistance to you.
Please feel free to contact me or Kenneth Tartaglino at (415)
937-8000 with your questions or comments.

Very truly yours,

McCUTCHEN, DOYLE, BROWN & ENERSEN

By [signature]
Michael P. Durkee

MPD:KJT:dlg/4
2448Q

cc: Commissioner Mikolajcik
Napa County LAFCO Commissioners
LAFCO Executive Officer
County Counsel' Office
American Canyon Incorporation Committee

FPFC

MAR 2 2:28 PM '90                                February 27, 1990


John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, Ca 95814

Re: Napa County request, Napa County Local Agency Formation Commission
    member John Mikolajcik.

Dear Mr. Wallace;

I am writing you hoping to shed further light on the issue of Mr. Mikolajcik's conflict of
interest in the hearings and decisions regarding the incorporation of American Canyon City. At
the outset, I would like you to know that I am not opposed to the incorporation. I think however,
that it must be presented in such a way that it accomplishes the goal of self-determination by
the voters, and does not happen under a cloud of having been manipulated by individuals or
groups whose special interests have controlled the process, or might misrepresent the issues
involved.

Let me begin by adding to the facts presented to you earlier. Mr. Mikolajcik owns four parcels
in the proposed city, one of which is zoned Commercial Limited and has a very successful retail
nursery on it. The other three are vacant parcels, all zoned General Commercial. One is 2.5
acres and the other two contiguous parcels add up to nearly one acre.(see Map #1). They are all
on the "strip commercial" along Highway 29, which has been existing and growing for many
decades. This strip commercial is the only commercial area within the proposed city, and his
holdings comprise about 7% of the total. His vacant General Commercial parcels are a very high
percentage of the vacant general commercial property along Highway 29.

It is also important to know the role these parcels have in Mr. Mikolajcik's total financial
picture. His only place of business is the retail nursery. His major land holdings are the three
undeveloped parcels in the area of American Canyon, although he does own land in Pope Valley
and a weekend place in Lake County. It can be assumed that between the retail nursery and the
investment in the three vacant commercially zoned parcels, we have identified the majority of
his income and property.

I would like to comment on what I have heard; that you have not been called upon to review the
issue of incorporation and conflict of interest before. I look at your Regulations, 5.7502.1.a.D.,
and I see the concern with describing areas of consideration, or adding to them, in such matters
as redevelopment, etc.. Nothing could more accurately describe the job that LAFCO is charged
with when it sets the boundary of a proposed city. A city is nothing more than a multi-
purpose service area, designed to control the development and services within its boundaries.

In addition to this fact, in Napa County there is a voter-enacted growth limiting measure,
requiring that growth OUTSIDE OF CITIES comprise only 1% of the total county population per
year. The impact of this limiting measure is only now being felt in American Canyon. There

were, and still are, residentially zoned lands in the area. About half of them were exempted
from the growth initiative when it was enacted and they are now completely built out. Other
large residentially zoned parcels exist,( see Map #2) and they are affected by the 1% per year
rate.

Thus, without a city, Mr. Mikolajcik's financial future looks quite static, since his financial
future is connected to residential and commercial growth in the area. As you may know, new
cities carry the previous zoning into their territory, and thus the presumption is that
previously zoned residential lands will remain zoned for residential development. In fact,a
developer has submitted plans for one large parcel. As of now, his development must be staged
to fit into the 1% yearly cap.

Given this voter-enacted measure which provides that residential growth belongs in cities, the
creation of another city, or even the addition of acreage to existing cities, is a very important
function of LAFCO in Napa County.

Let me now turn to the commercial and residential "community" which will be affected by the
setting of the boundaries of this incorporation. The proposed city has been connected to urban
type commercial activities occurring along the highway, and any additional shopping areas are
seen by the residents to be in Vallejo, in Solano County to the south. While their children attend
Napa schools, they do not look north to Napa for their major commercial activities. The
markets, banks, food services etc. along the highway are their "city" shopping areas, and the
rest occurs to the south in Solano County. Thus, the general commercial "community" is self
-contained for the most part, within American Canyon. One could argue that all residents of
Napa County will be affected if a new growth center develops which is not going to strengthen the
commercial areas in existing cities, but will require all county and educational services.

Napa County has an excess of industrially zoned land, located in better places, so the
presumption is that American Canyon City will be residential and commercial if it is to be
responsive to the market. It should also be noted that every attempt at incorporation has been
funded by residential and commercial developers.

The city boundaries being proposed include parcels now not zoned for residential uses, but
owned by people supporting the incorporation, since they see a greater profit in residences than
in agriculture. In addition, much of the industrially zoned land within the boundaries is
grossly under-developed or is vacant, and given the market and the amount of better
industrially zoned land, it is reasonable to assume that the new city could hope for industrial
development, but would be facing residential proposals. I have marked those properties on Map
#3.

All of this is intended to show that the setting of the boundaries, as well as the act of establishing
a proposal of a city, will greatly increase the "reasonable foreseeability" that residential
growth will be greatly encouraged by the creation of American Canyon City.

When your financial support is a retail nursery, and your land holdings are a significant
percentage of the vacant commercial land in the proposed city, how can it be possible that you
are not in conflict of interest? Were this a redevelopment area, the decision would already have
been made.

Finally, the question whether voters participation removes the conflict of interest on behalf of
a major property owner. I believe that it makes the conflict of interest even more possible. If

the boundaries of the proposed city(service district, redevelopment area) are drawn to include
lands which the voters think will be revenue-producing, such as industrially undeveloped or
under utilized lands, the revenue projections are thrown off. When the LAFCO official wants the
voters to approve the city including its projected budget needs, then he will set the boundaries
in such a way that the economic projections are overly optimistic.

The truth is, there is a direct connection between what the boundary setters (LAFCO) decide,
and the message that will be sent to the voter's by the proponents of incorporation.

In summary, given Mr. Mikolajcik's holdings, and the fact that LAFCO sets the boundaries which
can effect the voter's outcome, and the fact that, in Napa County, any city at all results in faster
residential development, it would seem to me that he has a conflict of interest.

If you have any questions, I can be reached at 707-257-2389 or 707-224-2105.

Thank you for your consideration of this letter.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Mrs. Ginny Simms
210ak Grove Way
Napa, CA 94559

cc: David L. Zaltzman, Deputy County Counsel

EVELOPED PARCEL REZONED TO GENERAL COMMERCIAL

The map shows what appears to be an urban planning or zoning document with hatched/shaded areas in red in certain sections on the right side of the map.]

AMERICAN CANYON
NAPA COUNTY

MAP #3

NAPA COUNTY

OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL

ROBERT WESTMEYER, County Counsel

1195 THIRD STREET, ROOM 301 • NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94559-3001
AREA CODE 707/253-4521

MARGARET L. WOODBURY, Chief Deputy
JOSEPH C. FOLKARD, Deputy
DAVID L. ZALTSMAN, Deputy
NANCY STUART GRISHAM, Deputy
LAURA STITT SHEEDY, Deputy

February 20, 1990

OUR FILE NO.:

John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Informal Assistance/Napa County Local
Agency Formation Commission member John Mikolajcik

Dear Mr. Wallace:

This letter is to confirm and follow up on our telephone conversation of
last week with respect to the above-captioned topic. I am the Deputy County
Counsel who represents the Napa County Local Agency Formation Commission
(hereinafter "LAFCO") and have been requested by Commissioner Mikolajcik to
seek this advice. Initially, I should note that we will be content with
informal assistance, although we would like a written response. However, as
will be related below, the advice sought involves a series of specific
pending decisions. Therefore, if you prefer to handle this as a request for
formal written advice, that would be fine. Our only constraint is time:
the first and most critical meeting where action may be taken by the
Commission is set for March 14, 1990.

The remainder of this letter will consist of two brief sections. First, the
essential facts which you will need to analyze the issues raised. Second, I
will be providing some law and facts regarding the Napa County LAFCO which
this office feels is relevant to your analysis. The purpose of that
discussion will be to point out certain facts about LAFCO and the
incorporation process which, when applied to the three (3) aspects of
Government Code Section 87103, you will need to take into account prior to
rendering your opinion as to whether or not Commissioner Mikolajcik has a
disqualifying conflict of interest.

Finally, you should be aware that while Commissioner Mikolajcik would very
much like to participate in a process which he sees as facilitating his
constituents right to self government (see facts below) certain individuals
that are expected to be opposed to incorporation have raised the conflict
issue. Thus, your opinion needs to be in writing and will be closely
scrutinized by all sides.

John W. Wallace
February 20, 1990
Page 2

FACTS

Napa County has a population of approximately 108,900. Nearly 78,100 of
these people already live in incorporated areas (cities). Of the remaining
30,000 in the unincorporated area, approximately 7200 persons, which is 6%
of the County's total population, live in a geographically small and densely
populated community known as American Canyon, which is the subject of this
request for assistance (Source: ABAG Projections 1990). There has been
ongoing interest in American Canyon in incorporating itself community so as
to no longer be governed by the County. Last month an incorporation
committee submitted a petition for "reorganization" to the Napa County LAFCO
pursuant to the Cortese-Knox Local Government Reorganization Act of 1985
(Government Code Section 56000 et seq.; all future section references will
be to the Government Code.) The petition was for "reorganization" since it
included not only the incorporation of a city, but also the establishment of
one of the current independent districts providing services to the community
as a subsidiary district of the city. This means the new city council would
govern the district instead of an independent Board of Directors. The
petition contained 1,551 qualified signatures of the 3,625 potential
registered voters in the relevant area, well over the twenty-five percent
(25%) required by Section 56750(a).

LAFCO Commissioner Mikolajcik is also a member of the Board of Supervisors
of Napa County. He represents and lives in the American Canyon area and is
an ardent supporter of the incorporation effort on the Board of
Supervisors. In addition to owning his single family residence in the area
that is proposed for incorporation, Commissioner Mikolajcik also owns three
undeveloped parcels of property in the area and is the owner of a large,
successful nursery business in American Canyon.

There are approximately eighty (80) parcels of property in the American
Canyon area (in addition to Commissioner Mikolajcik's nursery business),
within Napa County's General Commercial Zoning District as shown on the
attached three (3) maps by shading (Exhibits "A" - "C"). As you can see,
nearly all of these parcels are in close proximity to each other and along
the State Highway 29 corridor. Also attached for your reference (Exhibit
"D") is a copy of the specifications and allowable uses for the General
Commercial District as contained in Sections 12245 - 12248.2 of the Napa
County Code.

One final point bears emphasis. The unincorporated area of Napa County is
subject to a growth control initiative known as "Measure A." Measure "A"
severely restricts the number of residential building permits that may be
issued in any given year (i.e., commercial and industrial development in the
unincorporated area is unaffected by Measure "A"). Any area which is
incorporated would no longer be subject to Measure "A". Theoretically, this
would mean that to the extent the newly incorporated area has developable
land, vacant or underdeveloped, it could - if the city council plans
accordingly - grow much more quickly than if the area remains unincorporated
(see discussion of boundaries on page 4 of this letter).

John W. Wallace
February 20, 1990
Page 3

LAFCO AND THE INCORPORATION PROCESS

As you noted in your advice letter of November 1, 1989, in your file number
A-89-568:

"... Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 specifies
that an official has a financial interest if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally, on the official or a member of his
or her immediate family or on any real property in which the
public official has a direct or indirect interest worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental
decision is made depends on the facts of each particular
case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required; however, if an effect is only
a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198...."

Because of the peculiar nature of LAFCO and the incorporation process,
whether the action of LAFCO would have a foreseeable and/or material effect
on the real property or business of Commissioner Mikoljcik is at least an
open question and needs to be discussed in your opinion.

With respect to reasonable foreseeability, in our view it is important to
recognize that LAFCO itself does not, and cannot, actually change the
organization of the community from unincorporated to a city. Pursuant to
Section 56375(a) LAFCO has the power and duty:

To review and approve or disapprove with or without amendment, wholly, partially, or conditionally, proposals for
changes of organization or reorganization. (Emphasis
added.)

Even if LAFCO approves a proposal for incorporation of a city it must then
refer the matter to the "conducting authority", (in this case the Board of
Supervisors), who - if a majority written protest does not occur, which
would mandate that it abandon the reorganization (see sections 57077 and 57078)
- must set the question for an election of the affected populace. (See
Section 57077(b).) A majority of the affected population must approve of
the incorporation for it to become effective.

John W. Wallace
February 20, 1990
Page 4


More importantly, LARCOs have absolutely no power over land use (see Section
56375(a)(2); third paragraph). Decisions on land use within the new city
boundaries will be the responsibility of the new city council (or if the
incorporation election fails remain under the control of the Board of
Supervisors). What LARCO can affect, however, are the initial boundaries of
the new city. If LARCO approves the boundaries as currently proposed by the
incorporation proponents, much open land would be taken out of the
unincorporated area and therefore no longer be subject to the effect of
Measure A (discussed earlier). Theoretically, this area could then develop
much faster than if the area remained unincorporated; assuming the new city
council were so inclined. This might have an effect on the value of the
Commissioners property or the revenues of his business. However, it is
equally conceivable that a new city council will want to remain "slow
growth" and retain the current general plan designations on the open space.
Equally plausible is the possibility that LARCO may substantially reduce the
boundaries of the proposed city to the point where little developable land
is included. If you conclude that a conflict exists due to the amount of
open space to be included in the proposed city, it would be very helpful if
your opinion would also discuss whether the conflict would continue to exist
subsequent to the point at which LARCO reduced the boundaries to exclude
virtually all undeveloped land proposed to be included in the proposed city
other than infill parcels.

The same principles would seem to apply to an analysis of materiality.
While we recognize the existence of Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) and (3)(A), we
note that Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(A) does not apply to incorporations. We
do not know whether this was an oversight on the part of the FPPC or whether
the FPPC felt the regulation should not apply to incorporations because
(1) typically involve a much larger area than an annexation (thus by
definition involving a significant segment of the public), and (2) always
require a vote of the people and subsequent action by a new city council
prior to any change occurring, (thus eliminating the possibility that the
effect on a LARCO Commissioner's property is reasonably foreseeable),
whereas annexations and the like rarely if ever require a vote of the
people. Additionally, we note that the exclusion found in Section
18702.1(a)(3)(B) seems more analogous to an incorporation and might override
the implications of Section 18702.1(a)(3)(A) although it would not seem to
affect the seemingly mandatory finding of materiality set forth in Section
18702.1(b)(2). It bears repeating that LARCO's action, does not itself
create a city, but merely allows the voters to approve incorporation.

Finally, if you conclude that a conflict does exist because Commissioner
Mikolajcik's economic interests will be benefited, the question of whether
or not he should nevertheless be entitled to vote because the effect on his
economic interest will not be distinguishable from its effect on a
significant segment of the public must be addressed. This would seem to
raise two issues. First, what is the "public" in the case of LARCO?
Second, since Commissioner Mikolajcik is a retail merchant, homeowner and
owner of vacant property in American Canyon do those groups constitute a
significant segment of the public which will be impacted in substantially
the same way as Commissioner Mikolajcik?

John W. Wallace
February 20, 1990
Page 5


In considering the first question, please keep in mind that although
theoretically Napa County's LAFCO has county-wide jurisdiction, there are
virtually no powers or duties which LAFCO possesses which impact the cities
where seventy percent (70%) of Napa County's population live. Additionally,
LAFCO's membership is hybrid consisting of two city councilmembers, two
members of the County Board of Supervisors and one public at large member.
We are unaware of any FPPC opinions that discuss how one determines what
constitutes the "public generally" in such cases.

In regard to the second question, please review In Re Owen 2 FPPC Opinions
77, 81-83, which we believe might be controlling. That opinion stated that
retail merchants and homeowners located in or near the city core of the City
of Davis as a group constituted a significant segment of the public even
though that group by definition did not constitute all homeowners or
merchants in the City of Davis. This would seem to be the case here since a
significant number of retail businesses are involved but not all businesses
in the County of Napa (this is especially the case if the incorporated areas
are not considered since the County has very little retail businesses
outside of the American Canyon area). On the other hand, the matter is not
free from doubt until the "community" is determined, since many previously
issued opinions of the FPPC have indicated that less than 50% of the retail
businesses in a community did not affect a significant segment of the
public. In Re Brown 4 FPPC 19, but that 72% did. (In re Ragsone Notice
Letter No. A-85-039.) Presumeably the owners of vacant real property would
fall into the same category as homeowners and the result would therefore be
the same regarding Commissioner Mikolajcik's vacant property. If you
conclude that Commissioner Mikolajcik has a conflict and if you believe the
Owens rationale does not apply in this case, an explanation of why it does
not apply would be helpful in our task of explaining to Commissioner
Mikolajcik why he may have a conflict of interest.

I hope the above has been of some help to you. If you have any questions,
please feel free to contact me. I look forward to hearing from you in the
near future.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

DAVID L. ZALTSMAN,
Deputy County Counsel

sections.conclusion:
Commissioner Mikolajcik should be permitted to participate in the LAFCO decision regarding the American Canyon incorporation. LAFCO does not have the power to organize an unincorporated area into a city. LAFCO approval merely allows the incorporation process to continue. Ultimately, the affected voters in the area will either approve or disapprove the incorporation. Further, LAFCO does not possess land use policy or regulatory powers (the powers which materially effect financial interests), it merely takes actions which may lead to the establishment of such policies and regulations by other governmental entities. Whatever financial effects may flow to the Commissioner's property or business are mere possibilities at this stage and cannot be considered reasonably foreseeable, are not material, and are not distinguishable from effects on the public generally. Thus, the Act does not require that the Commissioner disqualify himself.

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990

We hope this letter will be of assistance to you. Please feel free to contact me or Kenneth Tartaglino at (415) 937-8000 with your questions or comments.
sections.facts:
Napa County has a population of approximately 108,900. Nearly 78,100 of these people already live in incorporated areas (cities). Of the remaining 30,000 in the unincorporated area, approximately 7200 persons, which is 6% of the County's total population, live in a geographically small and densely populated community known as American Canyon, which is the subject of this request for assistance (Source: ABAG Projections 1990). There has been ongoing interest in American Canyon in incorporating their community so as to no longer be governed by the County. Last month an incorporation committee submitted a petition for "reorganization" to the Napa County LAFCO pursuant to the Cortese-Knox Local Government Reorganization Act of 1985 (Government Code Section 56000 et seq.; all future section references will be to the Government Code.) The petition was for "reorganization" since it included not only the incorporation of a city, but also the establishment of one of the current independent districts providing services to the community as a subsidiary district of the city. This means the new city council would govern the district instead of an independent Board of Directors. The petition contained 1,551 qualified signatures of the 3,625 potential registered voters in the relevant area, well over the twenty-five percent (25%) required by Section 56750(a).

LAFCO Commissioner Mikolajcik is also a member of the Board of Supervisors of Napa County. He represents and lives in the American Canyon area and is an ardent supporter of the incorporation effort on the Board of Supervisors. In addition to owning his single family residence in the area that is proposed for incorporation, Commissioner Mikolajcik also owns three undeveloped parcels of property in the area and is the owner of a large, successful nursery business in American Canyon.

There are approximately eighty (80) parcels of property in the American Canyon area (in addition to Commissioner Mikolajcik's nursery business), within Napa County's General Commercial Zoning District as shown on the attached three (3) maps by shading (Exhibits "A" - "C"). As you can see, nearly all of these parcels are in close proximity to each other and along the State Highway 29 corridor. Also attached for your reference (Exhibit "D") is a copy of the specifications and allowable uses for the General Commercial District as contained in Sections 12245 - 12248.2 of the Napa County Code.

One final point bears emphasis. The unincorporated area of Napa County is subject to a growth control initiative known as "Measure A." Measure "A" severely restricts the number of residential building permits that may be issued in any given year (i.e., commercial and industrial development in the unincorporated area is unaffected by Measure "A"). Any area which is incorporated would no longer be subject to Measure "A". Theoretically, this would mean that to the extent the newly incorporated area has developable land, vacant or underdeveloped, it could - if the city council plans accordingly - grow much more quickly than if the area remains unincorporated (see discussion of boundaries on page 4 of this letter).

LAFCO AND THE INCORPORATION PROCESS

As you noted in your advice letter of November 1, 1989, in your file Number A-89-568:

"... Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required; however, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198...."

Because of the peculiar nature of LAFCO and the incorporation process, whether the action of LAFCO would have a foreseeable and/or material effect on the real property or business of Commissioner Mikoljcik is at least an open question and needs to be discussed in your opinion.

With respect to reasonable foreseeability, in our view it is important to recognize that LAFCO itself does not, and cannot, actually change the organization of the community from unincorporated to a city. Pursuant to Section 56375(a) LAFCO has the power and duty:

To review and approve or disapprove with or without amendment, wholly, partially, or conditionally, proposals for changes of organization or reorganization. (Emphasis added.)

Even if LAFCO approves a proposal for incorporation of a city it must then refer the matter to the "conducting authority", (in this case the Board of Supervisors), who - if a majority written protest does not occur, which would mandatorily abandon the reorganization (see sections 57077 and 57078) - must set the question for an election of the effected populace. (See Section 57077(b).) A majority of the effected population must approve of the incorporation for it to become effective.

More importantly, LAFCOs have absolutely no power over land use (see Section 56375(a)(2); third paragraph). Decisions on land use within the new city boundaries will be the responsibility of the new city council (or if the incorporation election fails remain under the control of the Board of Supervisors). What LAFCO can affect, however, are the initial boundaries of the new city. If LAFCO approves the boundaries as currently proposed by the incorporation proponents, much open land would be taken out of the unincorporated area and therefore no longer be subject to the effect of Measure A (discussed earlier). Theoretically, this area could then develop much faster than if the area remained unincorporated; assuming the new city council were so inclined. This might have an effect on the value of the Commissioners property or the revenues of his business. However, it is equally conceivable that a new city council will want to remain "slow growth" and retain the current general plan designations on the open space. Equally plausible is the possibility that LAFCO may substantially reduce the boundaries of the proposed city to the point where little developable land is included. If you conclude that a conflict exists due to the amount of open space to be included in the proposed city, it would be very helpful if your opinion would also discuss whether the conflict would continue to exist subsequent to the point at which LAFCO reduced the boundaries to exclude virtually all undeveloped land proposed to be included in the proposed city other than infill parcels.

The same principles would seem to apply to an analysis of materiality. While we recognize the existence of Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) and (3)(A), we note that Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(A) does not apply to incorporations. We do not know whether this was an oversight on the part of the FPPC or whether the FPPC felt the regulation should not apply to incorporations because they (1) typically involve a much larger area than an annexation (thus by definition involving a significant segment of the public), and (2) always require a vote of the people and subsequent action by a new city council prior to any change occurring, (thus eliminating the possibility that the effect on a LAFCO Commissioner's property is reasonably foreseeable), whereas annexations and the like rarely if ever require a vote of the people. Additionally, we note that the exclusion found in Section 18702.1(a)(3)(E) seems more analogous to an incorporation and might override the implications of Section 18702.1(a)(3)(A) although it would not seem to affect the seemingly mandatory finding of materiality set forth at Section 18702.1(a)(2). It bears repeating that LAFCO's action, does not itself create a city, but merely allows the voters to approve incorporation.

Finally, if you conclude that a conflict does exist because Commissioner Mikolajcik's economic interests will be benefited, the question of whether or not he should nevertheless be entitled to vote because the effect on his economic interest will not be distinguishable from its effect on a significant segment of the public must be addressed. This would seem to raise two issues. First, what is the "public" in the case of LAFCO? Second, since Commissioner Mikolajcik is a retail merchant, homeowner and owner of vacant property in American Canyon do those groups constitute a significant segment of the public which will be impacted in substantially the same way as Commissioner Mikolajcik?

In considering the first question, please keep in mind that although theoretically Napa County's LAFCO has county-wide jurisdiction, there are virtually no powers or duties which LAFCO possesses which impact the cities where seventy percent (70%) of Napa County's population live. Additionally, LAFCO's membership is hybrid consisting of two city councilpersons, two members of the County Board of Supervisors and one public at large member. We are unaware of any FPPC opinions that discuss how one determines what constitutes the "public generally" in such cases.

In regard to the second question, please review In Re Owen 2 FPPC Opinions 77, 81-83, which we believe might be controlling. That opinion stated that retail merchants and homeowners located in or near the city core of the City of Davis as a group constituted a significant segment of the public even though that group by definition did not constitute all homeowners or merchants in the City of Davis. This would seem to be the case here since significant number of retail businesses are involved but not all businesses in the County of Napa (this is especially the case if the incorporated areas are not considered since the County has very little retail businesses outside of the American Canyon area). On the other hand, the matter is not free from doubt until the "community" is determined, since many previously issued opinions of the FPPC have indicated that less than 50% of the retail businesses in a community did not affect a significant segment of the public, In Re Brown 4 FPPC 19, but that 72% did. (In re Waggoner Advice Letter No. A-85-089.) Presumably the owners of vacant real property would fall into the same category as homeowners and the result would therefore be the same regarding Commissioner Mikolajcik's vacant property. If you conclude that Commissioner Mikolajcik has a conflict and if you believe the Owens rationale does not apply in this case, an explanation of why it does not apply would be helpful in our task of explaining to Commissioner Mikolajcik why he may have a conflict of interest.

I hope the above has been of some help to you. If you have any questions, please feel free to contact me. I look forward to hearing from you in the near future.
sections.analysis:
A. Background.

As the County Counsel's office outlined in its letter, our client has submitted a petition to the Napa County LAFCO proposing the incorporation of the American Canyon area of the County pursuant to the Cortese-Knox Local Government Reorganization Act of 1985. Government Code1 Section 56000 et seq. The Commissioner represents and lives in the American Canyon area, where he owns a residence, certain undeveloped property and a nursery business.

B. Applicable Standard.

The Political Reform Act provides that "no public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in the making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." Section 87100.

However, a number of elements must exist before the Act requires a public official to disqualify himself from participation in a governmental decision: First, it must be reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a financial effect. Second, the anticipated financial effect must be on a financial interest of the official, and it must be material. And third, the anticipated financial effect must be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. See, In re Thorner, 1 FPPC 198, 202 (1975).

1 All section references are to the California Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

Each of these elements are discussed below.

1. Foreseeability.

Before the Commissioner must disqualify himself from voting on the proposed American Canyon incorporation, it must be "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a material effect on one of these interests. The preliminary nature of the LAFCO decision and the particular facts surrounding the Commissioner's situation suggest that no material financial effect on these interests is reasonably foreseeable.

Whether an effect is foreseeable depends on the particular facts and circumstances of each situation. See, Thorner, supra. A financial effect is considered reasonably foreseeable when there is a substantial likelihood or probability that it will occur. Certainty is not required; however, if the financial effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. Id.

An understanding of the incorporation process and LAFCO's limited powers is key to understanding why the LAFCO decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable effect on the Commissioner's financial interests. First, LAFCO's decision will not be the final determinant of the American Canyon incorporation. LAFCO does not have the power to organize an unincorporated area into a city. Instead, LAFCO's approval only serves to forward the proposal to the "conducting authority," which then must determine if the proposed incorporation should be placed before the effected citizens. See, Sections 57077, 57078. The voters residing in the proposed incorporation area render the final decision on incorporation.

Second, LAFCO has no power over existing or subsequent land use policies and regulations regarding the proposed incorporation area (or any other area). Section 56375(a)(2). If the proposed incorporation is rejected by the voters, control over land use will remain with the Board of Supervisors; if the incorporation is approved, the new city council will exercise that power. In either case, LAFCO will not play a policy-making role regarding land use issues.

Because the voters, not LAFCO, will determine whether the new city is established, and because LAFCO has no power to control land use, any financial effect which may indirectly

flow from LAFCO's decision on the incorporation, is a "mere possibility" and too remote to be considered reasonably foreseeable.

2. Financial Interest and Materiality.

Under the Act, a decision's financial effect is considered "material" in one of two ways. First, Section 18702.1 of Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations ("Regis.") lists specific situations which have been generally determined to be material. Second, when Section 18702.1 cannot be applied, a decision will be said to have a material effect "if the decision will have a significant effect" on the official or his financial interests. Regs. Section 18702(b).

a. Real Property.

Section 18702.1(a)(3) outlines situations where the decision will materially effect the official's interests in real property. This section specifically mentions decisions involving annexation or deannexation but fails to include those involving incorporations. A likely explanation for the omission is that incorporations typically involve a much larger area than annexations and thus involve a significant segment of the public by definition. In addition, an incorporation requires a vote of the effected population. Thus, the effect of any potential conflict can be rectified by the voters.

Assuming that the LAFCO decision is not covered by Section 18702.1 and thus its effect is not material by definition, the decision's effect is material only if it will have a "significant effect" on the Commissioner's residence or undeveloped parcels. Again, because LAFCO cannot incorporate American Canyon or control land use within the American Canyon area, its decision will not significantly effect the Commissioner's real property interests. Additional actions on the part of American Canyon residents and a city council not yet in existence must occur before the possibility of a significant effect will occur.

b. Business Investment.

The Commissioner's nursery business is a business entity in which the Commissioner has a direct investment of more than $1,000 and in which he has a position of management. Section 18702.1(a)(2). However, as discussed below, the

anticipated effect of the decision will not be distinguishable from the public generally because the nursery is but one of many retail businesses located in the proposed incorporation area. Therefore, no conflict exists.

3. Public Generally Distinction.

Finally, before the Act requires an official to disqualify himself, the "decision's anticipated financial effect on the official's financial interest must be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally." Thorner, at 202, emphasis in original. Thus, even if you determine that the LAFCO decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material effect on the Commissioner's property and business, a conflict will not exist if the effect is not distinguishable from the public generally.

a. Real Property.

The Commissioner owns a single family residence and some vacant land in the proposed incorporation area. However, a conflict does not exist. The Commissioner, as a landowner, is a member of a group which constitutes a "significant segment" of the public. Thus the effect of the LAFCO decision on his property will not be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. In re Owen, 2 FPPC 77 (1976).

In Owen, a planning commissioner, who owned a home on the border of a core area, was allowed to participate in decisions involving the core area's specific land use plan. Id. at 78. The FPPC reasoned that:

While certain aspects of the plan may operate to increase the value of this home, either as residential or commercial property, it is apparent that the plan will have a "substantially similar" effect on numerous other residential properties, perhaps throughout the entire city as well as those near the core area.

Id. at 81.

In the instant case, while LAFCO's decision may operate to increase the value of the Commissioner's land, either as residential or commercial property, it will have a substantially similar effect on all other properties and property owners in the proposed incorporation area (and possibly outside American Canyon as well).

b. The Nursery.

Again, the reasoning in Owen is controlling. In Owen, the FPPC also determined that a council member, a retail merchant, could participate in the adoption of a specific land use plan for the City of Davis even though his store was located in the plan's core area. The opinion stated that "merchants constitute a major part of what is generally regarded as the business community and taken as a whole, may reasonably be regarded as 'the public generally' within the meaning of Section 87103 and our regulations." Id. at 82. It is important to note that the core area merchants did not represent all merchants in the City of Davis. The FPPC also recognized that there was no indication that the plan would have a particularly beneficial or harmful effect on the council member's particular business. Ibid.

Under the reasoning of Owen, the Commissioner's nursery falls within "the public generally" distinction. The American Canyon business community represents "the public generally;" very little retail business exists outside the proposed incorporation area and a significant number of retail businesses exist within the proposed borders. In addition, there is no indication that the LAFCO decision will affect the Commissioner's nursery any differently than the other businesses. Finally, the effect of the possible incorporation on the Commissioner's nursery business, an existing and ongoing concern, is likely to be minimal.

CONCLUSION: Commissioner Mikolajcik should be permitted to participate in the LAFCO decision regarding the American Canyon incorporation. LAFCO does not have the power to organize an unincorporated area into a city. LAFCO approval merely allows the incorporation process to continue. Ultimately, the affected voters in the area will either approve or disapprove the incorporation. Further, LAFCO does not possess land use policy or regulatory powers (the powers which materially effect financial interests), it merely takes actions which may lead to the establishment of such policies and regulations by other governmental entities. Whatever financial effects may flow to the Commissioner's property or business are mere possibilities at this stage and cannot be considered reasonably foreseeable, are not material, and are not distinguishable from effects on the public generally. Thus, the Act does not require that the Commissioner disqualify himself.

John W. Wallace, Esq.
March 6, 1990

We hope this letter will be of assistance to you. Please feel free to contact me or Kenneth Tartaglino at (415) 937-8000 with your questions or comments.

Legal Division California Fair Political Practices Commission 428 "J" Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Conflict of Interest of Napa County LAFCO Commissioner Our File No. 89000-001 Dear Mr. Wallace: We represent the American Canyon Incorporation Committee in relation to its proposal to incorporate the American Canyon area of Napa County. That proposal is currently before the Napa County Local Agency Formation Commission ("LAFCO") for review, prior to being sent to the voters for action. It is our understanding that the Napa County Counsel's office has requested your assistance concerning the California Political Reform Act (the "Act") as it may apply to LAFCO Commissioner John Mikolajcik and his involvement in LAFCO actions on that incorporation proposal. For the reasons set forth below, we believe that no conflict of interest exists and that Commissioner Mikolajcik should be allowed to participate in all LAFCO actions relative to the incorporation. I. SUMMARY Commissioner Mikolajcik should be permitted to participate in the LAFCO decision regarding the American Canyon incorporation. LAFCO does not have the power to organize an unincorporated area into a city. LAFCO approval merely allows the incorporation process to continue. Ultimately, the affected voters in the area will either approve or disapprove John W. Wallace, Esq. March 6, 1990 Page 2 the incorporation. Further, LAFCO does not possess land use policy or regulatory powers (the powers which materially effect financial interests), it merely takes actions which may lead to the establishment of such policies and regulations by other governmental entities. Whatever financial effects may flow to the Commissioner's property or business are mere possibilities at this stage and cannot be considered reasonably foreseeable, are not material, and are not distinguishable from effects on the public generally. Thus, the Act does not require that the Commissioner disqualify himself. II. ANALYSIS A. Background.
